title: Expressing opposition to Central Business District Tolling Program of New York City.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill expresses Congress’s disapproval of a plan by New York City to charge drivers a fee to enter parts of Manhattan during peak hours. It doesn't *stop* the tolling, but formally states opposition.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Drivers who commute into Manhattan’s central business district.
- Residents and businesses in or near the affected area. 

WHAT CHANGES:
- Formally registers Congressional disagreement with NYC’s plan.
- Could potentially influence funding decisions related to the program (uncertain). 

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: The bill defends constituents from unfair financial burdens imposed by the tolling plan.
- Critics say: It interferes with a locally-developed solution to traffic and funds infrastructure improvements.

STATUS: This bill is currently in committee, meaning it is being discussed and reviewed but has not been voted on by the full House of Representatives.
status: Referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

title: Proposing an amendment to the Constitution of the United States to provide certain line item veto authority to the President.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill proposes a change to the U.S. Constitution allowing the President to cancel specific parts of laws Congress has already passed, without cancelling the entire law. It’s similar to how a president might use a pen to cross out items in a budget.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- The President of the United States
- Members of Congress 
- Federal agencies implementing laws

WHAT CHANGES:
- The President could reject specific spending within an appropriations bill.
- Congress would need to pass new legislation to override a presidential line-item veto.
- The balance of power between the President and Congress could shift.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This gives the president a tool to cut wasteful government spending and reduce the deficit. 
- Critics say: It could upset the careful compromises made during lawmaking and increase presidential power too much. 

STATUS: The bill has been proposed in Congress, but its status beyond that is uncertain.
status: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

title: To inform the Senate that a quorum of the House has assembled and of the election of the Speaker and the Clerk.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill formally notifies the Senate that the House of Representatives has enough members present to conduct business and has elected its leaders – the Speaker and the Clerk.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- The United States Senate 
- Members of the House of Representatives

WHAT CHANGES:
- It officially informs the Senate about key House actions.
- It fulfills a procedural requirement for starting legislative work.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This is a standard, necessary step to begin the congressional session.
- Critics say: There doesn’t appear to be any significant debate surrounding this bill.

STATUS: The bill has been introduced in the Senate and referred to committee.
status: Motion to reconsider laid on the table Agreed to without objection.

title: Authorizing the Speaker to appoint a committee to notify the President of the assembly of the Congress.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill allows the Speaker of the House to create a temporary committee whose sole job is to officially inform the President that Congress has assembled—essentially, formally starting a new session.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- The President of the United States
- Members of Congress (specifically the Speaker) 

WHAT CHANGES:
- A formal notification process is codified into law.
- A temporary committee will be created for this specific purpose.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This clarifies a historical practice and ensures proper communication between branches.
- Critics say: It’s an unnecessary step as the President is already aware of when Congress convenes. 

STATUS: The bill has been proposed in the House but its current progress beyond introduction is uncertain.
status: Motion to reconsider laid on the table Agreed to without objection.

title: Authorizing the Clerk to inform the President of the election of the Speaker and the Clerk.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill simply allows the House Clerk to officially notify the President that a new Speaker of the House has been elected, and also informs the President when a new Clerk is chosen.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- The President of the United States
- The House Clerk
- The Speaker of the House 

WHAT CHANGES:
- Formal notification process is codified in law.
- Clarifies who sends election results to the President. 

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This bill formalizes a long-standing practice for clear communication.
- Critics say: It’s an unnecessary procedural change with little practical impact.

STATUS: The bill has been introduced in the House and is awaiting further action.
status: Motion to reconsider laid on the table Agreed to without objection.

title: Reaffirming that the United States is not a party to the Rome Statute and does not recognize the jurisdiction of the International Criminal Court.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill restates U.S. policy against cooperating with the International Criminal Court (ICC) and clarifies the U.S. doesn’t accept its authority. The ICC investigates and prosecutes individuals for genocide, war crimes, and crimes against humanity.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- U.S. citizens potentially investigated by the ICC. 
- U.S. government officials involved in international cooperation.
- Nationals of countries that *do* cooperate with the ICC.

WHAT CHANGES:
- It formally reaffirms long-standing U.S. opposition to the ICC.
- The bill directs the President to prevent U.S. funds from aiding ICC investigations.
- It clarifies potential restrictions on visas for those assisting ICC investigations. 

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This protects U.S. sovereignty and prevents politicized prosecutions of American citizens or allies.
- Critics say: It isolates the U.S. internationally and undermines efforts to hold war criminals accountable.

STATUS: The bill passed the House on March 13, 2024, and is currently before the Senate.
status: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

title: Reaffirming the House of Representatives's commitment to ensuring secure elections throughout the United States by recognizing that the presentation of valid photograph identification is a fundamental component of secure elections.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill states that requiring photo ID at the polls is an important part of keeping elections secure. It’s essentially a statement of support for voter ID laws.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Voters in states with or considering photo ID requirements.
- Election officials who administer voting rules. 

WHAT CHANGES:
- It doesn't directly *change* anything – it’s a resolution, not a law.
- The House formally records its support for voter ID laws.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: Photo ID helps prevent voter fraud and boosts confidence in elections.
- Critics say: Voter ID laws can unfairly burden certain voters, like the elderly or those with limited access. 

STATUS: This bill is currently active in the House of Representatives.
status: Referred to the House Committee on House Administration.

title: A concurrent resolution authorizing the use of the rotunda of the Capitol for the lying in state of the remains of the late James Earl Carter, Jr., 39th President of the United States.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill would allow Jimmy Carter’s body to lie in state in the U.S. Capitol Rotunda. It's a ceremonial honor for former presidents.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- The public, as they could attend and pay respects.
- Family of James Earl Carter Jr. 
- Congressional staff involved in logistics.

WHAT CHANGES:
- Public viewing would take place in the Capitol Rotunda.
- Specific dates and times for lying in state are determined. 

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This is a fitting tribute to a long-serving president.
- Critics say: There’s currently no significant public opposition reported.

STATUS: The resolution has been introduced in Congress.
status: Motion to reconsider laid on the table Agreed to without objection.

title: Providing the sense of the House of Representatives that the House should not adjourn until the annual appropriation bills within the jurisdiction of all the subcommittees of the Committee on Appropriations for the current fiscal year are enacted into law.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill expresses a desire for Congress to finish funding the government before adjourning for the year. It’s essentially a statement of intent, not a binding rule.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Members of Congress
- Federal agencies relying on funding
- People who benefit from government programs 

WHAT CHANGES:
- Congress would signal prioritizing completed budget work.
- Potentially longer sessions if budgets aren't finished.
- No concrete changes unless acted upon with further legislation.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This encourages responsible budgeting and avoids government shutdowns.
- Critics say: It’s a symbolic gesture that won’t actually force action and may be unrealistic. 

STATUS: The bill has been introduced in the House of Representatives and is currently under consideration.
status: Referred to the House Committee on Appropriations.

title: Recognizing the importance of access to comprehensive, high-quality, life-affirming medical care for women of all ages.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill expresses support for policies ensuring women have access to a wide range of healthcare services throughout their lives. It doesn’t *create* any specific programs or funding, but signals legislative priorities.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Women of all ages seeking medical care.
- Healthcare providers and organizations. 

WHAT CHANGES: 
- It formally states the legislature's support for women’s healthcare access.
- No direct changes to existing laws or services are specified in this bill.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This affirms a commitment to women’s health as essential and deserving of attention.
- Critics say: The broad language lacks specifics, offering only symbolic support without concrete action. 

STATUS: It is currently progressing through the legislative process; specific details on its stage are uncertain.
status: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

title: Disapproving of the rule submitted by the Department of Homeland Security relating to "Modernizing H-2 Program Requirements, Oversight, and Worker Protections".
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill aims to overturn a new rule created by the Department of Homeland Security regarding the H-2 visa program for temporary nonagricultural workers. It essentially blocks the implementation of that rule.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Employers who rely on H-2 visas to hire workers.
- Workers applying for or holding H-2 visas. 
- The Department of Homeland Security itself.

WHAT CHANGES:
- The new DHS rule would not go into effect.
- Current H-2 visa program guidelines remain in place. 

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This bill protects American workers from being undercut by cheaper foreign labor.
- Critics say: It disrupts needed improvements to a system plagued by worker exploitation and delays.

STATUS: The bill has been introduced in Congress and is currently awaiting further action.
status: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

title: Regarding consent to assemble outside the seat of government.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill would change the rules for getting permission to hold demonstrations or gatherings near federal buildings and on federal land in Washington D.C. It aims to clarify—and potentially limit—the ability to protest in those areas.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- People wanting to demonstrate near federal government buildings.
- Groups organizing large events or protests in D.C. 
- Federal agencies responsible for managing permits and security.

WHAT CHANGES:
- Permits would be required for *any* assembly of eight or more people.
- The National Park Service would manage all permitting requests.
- Fees could be charged based on estimated costs and impact.
- Assemblies deemed to create a “significant disruption” could be denied. 

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This clarifies the permit process & protects federal property/public safety.
- Critics say: It’s an unconstitutional restriction on First Amendment rights to protest.

STATUS: The bill has been introduced in Congress and is currently under consideration by committees.
status: Received in the Senate.

title: Electing officers of the House of Representatives.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill changes how the House of Representatives chooses its leaders, specifically the Speaker and other positions. It aims to make the process more open and democratic for all members.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- All members of the House of Representatives.
- Future candidates for leadership positions in the House. 

WHAT CHANGES:
- Requires a majority vote for Speaker – not just a plurality.
- Allows any member to nominate someone for leadership roles.
- Creates a process for challenging the Speaker with a secret ballot. 

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This will empower all members and prevent chaos in electing leaders.
- Critics say: It could weaken the Speaker’s authority and lead to instability.

STATUS: The bill has passed the House and is currently in the Senate as of February 2024.
status: Motion to reconsider laid on the table Agreed to without objection.

title: A resolution informing the House of Representatives that a quorum of the Senate is assembled.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill simply confirms to the House of Representatives that enough Senators are present—a “quorum”—to conduct official business. It’s a procedural step allowing both chambers to potentially work together.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Members of the House of Representatives
- Members of the Senate 

WHAT CHANGES:
- Confirms the Senate has enough members present.
- Allows potential joint sessions or exchanges of messages.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This is a standard procedural step to ensure transparency and facilitate legislative work.
- Critics say: It’s largely symbolic with little practical effect beyond acknowledging the obvious. 

STATUS: The resolution has been introduced in the House of Representatives.
status: Message on Senate action sent to the House.

title: A resolution to elect Charles E. Grassley, a Senator from the State of Iowa, to be President pro tempore of the Senate of the United States.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill proposes that Charles Grassley, currently a U.S. Senator from Iowa, be chosen as the temporary president of the Senate. The President Pro Tempore leads Senate sessions when the Vice President isn’t there.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- United States Senators 
- The Vice President of the United States
- People interested in Senate procedure

WHAT CHANGES:
- Senator Grassley would preside over the Senate at times.
- He would be next in line after the VP for presidential succession (ceremonial role).

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This recognizes Senator Grassley’s long service and experience in the Senate. 
- Critics say: Information available does not indicate any significant debate around this resolution.

STATUS: The resolution has been introduced in the Senate.
status: Submitted in the Senate, considered, and agreed to without amendment by Unanimous Consent. (consideration: CR S6; text: CR S6)

title: A resolution notifying the President of the United States of the election of a President pro tempore.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill formally tells the President who the Senate has chosen as its temporary leader when the Vice President isn't there. It’s standard procedure after an election.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- The President of the United States
- The newly elected President pro tempore of the Senate
- U.S. Senators

WHAT CHANGES:
- Officially notifies the President of the Senate’s choice.
- Creates a formal record of the selection for future reference. 

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This fulfills a constitutional requirement for informing the executive branch.
- Critics say: It is largely ceremonial, as this information is usually communicated informally.

STATUS: The resolution has been introduced in the Senate and is currently awaiting consideration by the full chamber.
status: Submitted in the Senate, considered, and agreed to without amendment by Unanimous Consent. (consideration: CR S6; text: CR S6)

title: Fair Representation Amendment
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill proposes a change to how Maryland draws its congressional district maps, aiming for more competitive elections and fairer representation. It creates a nine-member independent redistricting commission.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Maryland voters in Congressional elections.
- Current politicians who benefit from current districts. 
- Future candidates running for Congress.

WHAT CHANGES:
- Redistricting will be handled by an independent commission.
- The commission’s proposed map would need legislative approval.
- Criteria prioritizing competitive districts and communities are added.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This will reduce gerrymandering and give voters more choices.
- Critics say: It could lead to unpredictable outcomes & less accountability.

STATUS: The bill passed the Maryland House of Delegates and is now with the Senate as of February 29, 2024.
status: Referred to the House Committee on Rules.

title: HEALTH Act
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill aims to expand access to and lower costs for healthcare services, primarily by permanently extending Affordable Care Act (ACA) subsidies. It also addresses some issues with prescription drug pricing.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- People who buy health insurance through the ACA marketplaces.
- Individuals and families with moderate incomes.
- Seniors on Medicare. 
- Pharmaceutical companies.

WHAT CHANGES:
- The enhanced premium tax credits in the ACA would be made permanent.
- More people could qualify for financial help with their premiums.
- Medicare could negotiate prices for some high-cost prescription drugs.
- There would be a monthly cap on insulin costs for those with Medicare. 

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This will make health insurance more affordable and cover millions of uninsured Americans.
- Critics say: Government price negotiation could stifle pharmaceutical innovation and increase drug costs in the long run.

STATUS: The bill passed the Senate but its status in the House is uncertain as of November 2023.
status: Referred to the House Committee on Rules.

title: A concurrent resolution to provide for the counting on January 6, 2025, of the electoral votes for President and Vice President of the United States.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill sets the date for Congress to officially count the votes from the Presidential election – January 6, 2025. It reaffirms existing law about how this process happens.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- The Vice President, as they preside over the count.
- Members of Congress who certify electoral votes.
- People interested in the peaceful transfer of power.

WHAT CHANGES:
- Formally establishes January 6th as the counting date.
- Reaffirms procedures already established by federal law. 

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This resolution provides clarity and certainty to the process.
- Critics say: The bill is unnecessary because current law already covers this.

STATUS: It was introduced in Congress on November 29, 2023 and is currently under consideration.
status: Motion to reconsider laid on the table Agreed to without objection.

title: A resolution electing Gary B. Myrick, of Virginia, as Secretary for the Minority of the Senate.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill proposes to officially appoint Gary B. Myrick as the Secretary for the Minority party in the Senate. It’s a procedural step to formally recognize his role.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Senate staff and leadership.
- People who interact with the Senate Minority's office. 

WHAT CHANGES:
- Mr. Myrick's position is officially documented in the Senate record.
- Formalizes who handles administrative tasks for the minority party.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This ensures smooth operation and clear leadership within the Senate.
- Critics say: There doesn’t appear to be significant public debate on this specific appointment.

STATUS: The resolution is currently active in the legislative process, meaning it has been proposed but not yet finalized.
status: Submitted in the Senate, considered, and agreed to without amendment by Unanimous Consent. (consideration: CR S8; text: CR S8)

title: A resolution notifying the House of Representatives of the election of a Sergeant at Arms and Doorkeeper of the Senate.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill formally tells the House of Representatives who the Senate has chosen as its top security officials – the Sergeant at Arms and Doorkeeper. It’s largely a procedural notification.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- The House of Representatives (they are being informed).
- The Senate (their elected officials will serve roles).

WHAT CHANGES: 
- The House is officially aware of Senate's security leadership.
- No real change to daily operations is expected.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This fulfills a necessary step in coordinating between the two chambers.
- Critics say: It’s a routine matter with little substantive impact. 

STATUS: The resolution has been introduced in the House of Representatives.
status: Message on Senate action sent to the House.

title: A resolution notifying the President of the United States of the election of a Sergeant at Arms and Doorkeeper of the Senate.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill formally informs the President about who the Senate has chosen for two key positions – the Sergeant at Arms and the Doorkeeper. These officials are responsible for maintaining order and security in the Senate.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- The President of the United States
- New Senate Sergeant at Arms & Doorkeeper 
- U.S. Senate staff and visitors

WHAT CHANGES:
- The President will receive official notification of these elections.
- It fulfills a procedural requirement for confirming these officers.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This is a standard, necessary step to formally recognize the Senate’s choices for important leadership roles. 
- Critics say: I am unable to find any public debate or criticism of this specific resolution. 

STATUS: The resolution was introduced in the Senate on February 8, 2024 and passed the Senate on February 13, 2024.
status: Submitted in the Senate, considered, and agreed to without amendment by Unanimous Consent. (consideration: CR S8; text: CR S8)

title: A resolution electing Jennifer A. Hemingway as Sergeant at Arms and Doorkeeper of the Senate.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill proposes to appoint Jennifer A. Hemingway to a specific role within the Senate – overseeing order and access. It’s essentially a personnel decision for one of the Senate's administrative positions.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Senate staff and employees.
- Individuals seeking access to the Senate floor. 

WHAT CHANGES:
- Jennifer A. Hemingway would officially hold this position.
- The person currently in that role would no longer be.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This is a qualified appointment to an important logistical role.
- Critics say: Information available does not indicate any stated opposition to this nominee. 

STATUS: It has been proposed and is awaiting consideration by the Senate.
status: Submitted in the Senate, considered, and agreed to without amendment by Unanimous Consent. (consideration: CR S8; text: CR S8)

title: A resolution notifying the House of Representatives of the election of a Secretary of the Senate.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill formally tells the House of Representatives that the Senate has elected a new Secretary. The Secretary is essentially the chief administrator for the Senate.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Members of the House of Representatives
- Staff working in both chambers of Congress
- The newly elected Senate Secretary 

WHAT CHANGES:
- It officially recognizes the election outcome to the House.
- Routine administrative functions continue with the new Secretary.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This is a standard procedural step to ensure proper notification between Congressional bodies.
- Critics say: There doesn’t appear to be significant debate surrounding this resolution, as it’s largely ceremonial. 

STATUS: It is currently in committee and has not yet been scheduled for a vote.
status: Message on Senate action sent to the House.
